title.title: The History of Blockchain
title.subtitle: How a movement for privacy and freedom gave birth to decentralized money

moneyHistory.heading: What Is Money?
moneyHistory.lead: Before we get to Bitcoin, a quick look at the long road that got us here. Every generation invented new money — and each form solved the limits of the last.
moneyHistory.calloutBody: Each form of money fixed a problem with the last — portability, scarcity, divisibility, settlement speed. Bitcoin was an attempt to fix the trust assumption itself.

moneyEvents.name: Barter
moneyEvents.desc: Direct exchange of goods. Worked in small tribes — broke down the moment someone had grain and the other wanted a roof.

moneyEvents.name: Commodity money
moneyEvents.desc: Shells, salt, cattle, beads. The first abstract store of value — items everyone agreed had worth.

moneyEvents.name: Coins (gold / silver)
moneyEvents.desc: Stamped metal coins. Scarce, durable, divisible. Empires rose and fell controlling the mint.

moneyEvents.name: Paper notes & banks
moneyEvents.desc: IOUs redeemable for gold, then for trust in the issuer alone. The gold standard ended in 1971 — money became pure trust.

moneyEvents.name: Bank cards / digital ledgers
moneyEvents.desc: Money becomes a database entry at a bank. Convenient — but every transaction passes through a gatekeeper.

moneyEvents.name: Bitcoin
moneyEvents.desc: Digital scarcity without a central issuer — the first form of money that nobody operates and nobody can switch off.

moneyValue.title: Why Does Money Have Value?
moneyValue.description: A piece of paper, a number in a database, a gold coin, a Bitcoin — none of them are intrinsically worth anything. Money works because four things are true at once.

pillars.label: Shared trust
pillars.desc: Everyone in the network agrees this thing is worth something. The agreement is the value.

pillars.label: Scarcity
pillars.desc: You can't easily make more of it. Gold is rare. Dollars are limited by central banks. Bitcoin caps at 21 million.

pillars.label: Utility
pillars.desc: You can use it — pay taxes, buy bread, settle a debt. A currency no one accepts is just a collectible.

pillars.label: Network effect
pillars.desc: The more people accept it, the more useful it becomes. New money struggles, established money compounds.

moneyValue.calloutBodyA: Tomorrow morning, every shop in your country decides not to accept euros. The notes in your wallet still exist — what are they worth? Money's value lives in
keyPoints: Money has value because we collectively decide it does — there is no objective number
When trust breaks (hyperinflation, bank runs, sanctions), the same notes lose worth overnight
Bitcoin's designers asked: can we engineer trust + scarcity + utility + network effects without any central authority?
This is the question the rest of this course tries to answer

cypherpunks.heading: The Cypherpunks
cypherpunks.lead: In the 1980s and 1990s, a loose group of cryptographers, hackers, and activists decided that privacy was not a luxury — it was a precondition for freedom.
cypherpunks.quote: "Privacy is a precondition for freedom, not a luxury."
cypherpunks.calloutBodyA: "Privacy is necessary for an open society in the electronic age. We cannot expect governments, corporations, or other large, faceless organizations to grant us privacy out of their beneficence." — Eric Hughes,

principles.title: Privacy by Default
principles.body: Systems should protect privacy without requiring trust in any authority — encryption is the default state, not a switch you have to find and turn on.

principles.title: Code is Law
principles.body: Cryptographic mathematics, not legislation, should enforce rights. A law can be repealed; a published cryptographic protocol cannot be un-known.

principles.title: Open Source
principles.body: Tools must be public, auditable, and available to everyone — no gatekeepers. If the code is secret, the privacy it claims to give is unverifiable.

principles.title: Build, Don't Beg
principles.body: Write code to change reality. Don't wait for permission from institutions, don't lobby — ship something that makes the lobbying irrelevant.

cypherpunkValues.title: Technology by the People, for the People
cypherpunkValues.description: The Cypherpunks weren't building products. They were building liberation tools — and they gave them away for free.
cypherpunkValues.wordPrivacySub: is a right, not a feature
cypherpunkValues.wordAutonomySub: over your own data and money
cypherpunkValues.wordCodeSub: is the law that can't be lobbied
cypherpunkValues.secondQuote: "We are writing code. We know that software can't be destroyed and that a widely dispersed system can't be shut down."
keyPoints: Anyone, anywhere could use or audit the tools — no permission needed
Designed to be censorship-resistant: no single point of shutdown
The enemy wasn't technology — it was centralised power over information
Bitcoin inherited this DNA: no CEO, no headquarters, no off switch

cypherpunksTimeline.heading: From Cypherpunks to Bitcoin
cypherpunksTimeline.lead: Twenty-seven years from the first anonymous digital cash to the genesis block — each link made the next one possible.

events.title: David Chaum — DigiCash
events.desc: First anonymous digital cash system using blind signatures. Chaum proved private electronic payments were mathematically possible — the cryptographic foundation everyone else would later build on.

events.title: Haber & Stornetta — Cryptographic Timestamps
events.desc: A cryptographically secured chain of blocks to timestamp digital documents. Without realizing it, they had invented the direct ancestor of every blockchain structure that followed.

events.title: Eric Hughes — A Cypherpunk's Manifesto
events.desc: The founding document of the cypherpunk movement. Privacy declared a fundamental right, to be defended with code instead of laws. The ideology Bitcoin would later embody.

events.title: Adam Back — Hashcash
events.desc: A proof-of-work system originally designed to combat email spam — forcing senders to burn small amounts of CPU. This exact concept became the direct inspiration for Bitcoin mining.

events.title: Wei Dai (B-Money) & Nick Szabo (Bit Gold)
events.desc: Two independent proposals for decentralized digital currencies using cryptographic proofs and distributed consensus. Both are now considered the direct precursors to Bitcoin's design.

events.title: Satoshi Nakamoto — The Bitcoin Whitepaper
events.desc: "Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System." An anonymous individual or group combines two decades of cypherpunk research — hashcash, chained timestamps, digital scarcity — into a single working protocol.

events.title: The Genesis Block
events.desc: January 3, 2009 — Block #0 is mined. Embedded message: "The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks". The ideological intent is written into the ledger itself.

pedigree.heading: Bitcoin's Academic Pedigree
pedigree.lead: The concept of cryptocurrencies is built from forgotten ideas in research literature — every piece predates Bitcoin by decades.
pedigree.imageAlt: Chronology of key ideas found in Bitcoin: a 1980-to-2015 grid mapping research strands (Linked Timestamping, Digital Cash, Proof of Work, Byzantine Fault Tolerance, Public Keys as Identities, Smart Contracts) to specific papers — Merkle Tree (1980), Ecash (1980s), Hashcash (1990s), Bit Gold (2005), Bitcoin (2008), Ethereum (2015).
pedigree.paperAuthors: Arvind Narayanan & Jeremy Clark · ACM Queue, 2017
pedigree.strandsHeader: Six research strands fed into Bitcoin

why.title: Why This History Matters
why.description: Bitcoin did not emerge from nothing. It was the culmination of 30 years of cryptographic research, failed experiments, and ideological conviction.
why.calloutBody: Satoshi didn't invent any single component. The genius was in combining existing cryptographic primitives with economic incentive design into one coherent system that actually worked.
keyPoints: Every core concept (hashing, digital signatures, PoW) predates Bitcoin
Previous attempts (DigiCash, B-Money, Bit Gold) all failed or remained theoretical
Bitcoin succeeded because it aligned cryptography with economic incentives
Understanding the history helps you evaluate new blockchain projects critically

whoControls.heading: Who Controls Your Money?
whoControls.lead: A side-by-side of what can actually happen to the value you hold, in each system.

rows.v: No — but can be lost, stolen, or seized in person
The bank holds it · you have a claim
Yes · bank can refuse transactions / clients
Platform holds · you have a balance
Yes · de-platformed, chargebacks, terms of service
Your keys, your coins (or a custodian, if you choose)
Not at the protocol layer · custodians can freeze theirs
Capped at 21 million · no human can change it
Same as a local transfer · minutes, no FX
Native-digital · settles on a public ledger

whoControls.questionA: What does it mean to truly
whoControls.questionTail: something digital — if there is always someone who can take it back?

takeaways.title: Prologue Takeaways
items: Money is older than writing — its form keeps changing, its function (trust + scarcity + utility + network) does not
Value lives in collective agreement, not in the object — when trust breaks, the same notes become paper
The cypherpunk movement championed privacy as a fundamental right, defended through code rather than law
From the 1980s to 2008, cryptographers built the individual pieces that Bitcoin would later unify
Satoshi combined proof-of-work, chained blocks, digital scarcity, and P2P networking — none of which he invented
The Genesis Block message — 'Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks' — reveals Bitcoin's ideological roots
Cash, bank accounts, and digital-payment platforms each hand control to someone else; Bitcoin asks: can you keep it yourself?

quizMoney.question: Money has taken many forms — shells, coins, paper, bank-account entries, Bitcoin. What is the underlying reason any of them have value?
options: The material they're made of (metal, paper, electrons) determines their worth
Governments declare them valuable through law
A network of people collectively agrees to accept them — trust, scarcity, utility, and network effects compound
Their value is fixed once a central authority decides on an exchange rate
quizMoney.explanation: Money's value is not in the object — it's in the shared agreement of everyone in the network. When that agreement breaks (hyperinflation, sanctions, platform bans), the same notes lose value overnight. The four foundations are: trust, scarcity, utility, and network effects. Bitcoin asks whether all four can be engineered without any single authority — that's the question this course explores.

cypherpunksTF.prompt: What did the Cypherpunks really stand for? Decide whether each claim is true or false.

statements.text: The Cypherpunks believed privacy is a precondition for a free society.
statements.explanation: This is the heart of the movement: without privacy, individuals cannot act freely. Eric Hughes' 1993 manifesto opens on exactly this idea.

statements.text: The Cypherpunks wanted governments to manage encryption on citizens' behalf.
statements.explanation: The opposite — they distrusted institutions and argued individuals should defend their own privacy directly, with cryptography rather than laws.

statements.text: Their motto 'Cypherpunks write code' meant software, not legislation, would protect privacy.
statements.explanation: They believed working code shipped into the world was more effective than lobbying for laws that could be ignored or reversed.

statements.text: Bitcoin appeared from nowhere, with no roots in earlier Cypherpunk work.
statements.explanation: Bitcoin built directly on decades of Cypherpunk research — Hashcash, b-money, digital signatures and more. It was an evolution, not a bolt from the blue.

statements.text: To the Cypherpunks, privacy meant the power to selectively reveal yourself to the world.
statements.explanation: Hughes defined privacy not as secrecy but as control: choosing what to reveal, to whom, and when.

quizCypherpunks.question: What was the core belief of the Cypherpunk movement?
options: Governments should regulate all digital communications
Privacy is a precondition for freedom and should be defended with code
Digital currencies should be managed by central banks
quizCypherpunks.explanation: The Cypherpunks believed that privacy is not a luxury but a fundamental right, and that cryptographic code — not laws or institutions — should enforce it.

quizHashcash.question: Which concept did Adam Back's Hashcash (1997) directly inspire in Bitcoin?
quizHashcash.explanation: Hashcash was a proof-of-work system originally designed to combat email spam. Satoshi Nakamoto adapted this concept as the foundation for Bitcoin's mining and consensus mechanism.

quizGenesis.question: What message did Satoshi Nakamoto embed in Bitcoin's Genesis Block?
options: "The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks"
quizGenesis.explanation: The embedded headline from The Times newspaper reveals Bitcoin's ideological roots — it was created as a direct response to the failures of the traditional banking system during the 2008 financial crisis.
